title: A year after distressed buyout, what's become of Bluebird Bio?
summary: David Meek, CEO of the former Bluebird Bio (now rebranded as Genetix), completed a distressed $30 million buyout of the nearly bankrupt company approximately one year prior to this July 2, 2026 report and claims a dramatic turnaround. Meek asserts Genetix is now leading the sickle cell disease gene therapy market — dismissing competitors aggressively in interview — and projects treating 1,000 patients annually by 2030. The company reportedly reached profitability within the year of the acquisition. One analyst described the trajectory as 'remarkable.' The full article body remains behind a STAT+ paywall, limiting deeper due diligence on clinical pipeline status, revenue figures, or competitive positioning versus Vertex and other gene therapy players.
analysis_notes: Article is behind STAT+ paywall; analysis based on headline, email preview text (shared marketing blurb), category/topic tags, and surrounding page context. CEO's claims of market dominance and profitability are self-serving assertions from an exclusive interview — no independent clinical or financial data available in accessible excerpt. Verify claims against SEC filings, pipeline trial registries, and third-party hematology analyses before acting on them.

In a swaggering  interview, one of the first he’s given since the deal, Meek lambasted competitors and claimed the company would dominate the market for sickle cell disease gene therapies for years to come. By 2030, he said the company — now rebranded as Genetix — would be treating 1,000 patients per year.

title: Anthropic CEO talks drug discovery, Medicare proposes steep 340B drug payment cuts, and more biotech news
summary:
STAT News rounds up several major biotech stories for July 6, 2026. Novartis agreed to acquire U.K.-based Myricx Bio for $1.1 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in milestone payments, gaining Myricx’s antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) platform designed for solid-tumor cancers and built to overcome limitations of existing ADC technologies. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei tempered expectations about AI transforming biotechnology, acknowledging that while AI could compress decades of scientific progress into years, biology’s inherent complexity means meaningful impact remains years away. Medicare proposed steep cuts to 340B drug reimbursement payments, a move with significant financial implications for hospitals and biotech firms dependent on the program. Republicans introduced legislation to preserve diversity in clinical trials, pushing the FDA to enforce stricter adherence to diversity requirements.

The article’s detailed content is behind a STAT+ paywall; the above summary is drawn from the freely-visible headline, bullet points, and surrounding context.

Happy post-fourth. Hope you had a nice extended weekend, and hi from San Francisco!

Today, we’re reading about Medicare proposing staggering cuts to 340B payments, Republicans moving to preserve diversity in clinical trials, and a former surgeon general, Jerome Adams, offering counsel on peptides regulation.

Advertisement

## The need-to-know this morning

- Novartis is [buying](https://www.novartis.com/news/media-releases/novartis-agrees-acquire-myricx-bio-advancing-next-generation-antibody-drug-conjugate-innovation-novel-nmti-payload-expanding-options-cancer-patients) the privately held Myricx Bio for $1.1 billion upfront, picking up an antibody-drug conjugate platform designed to create cancer-fighting drugs for different solid tumors and overcome some of the limitations of existing ADCs. The deal for the U.K.-based Myricx includes milestone payments of up to $400 million and [extends Novartis’ recent acquisition spree](https://www.statnews.com/2026/03/20/novartis-acquires-synnovation-breast-cancer-drug-snv4818/).

## Anthropic CEO understands biology’s stubborn limitations

Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei is tempering his own bold predictions about AI transforming biotechnology, STAT’s Matt Herper writes. While, sure, AI could eventually compress decades of scientific progress into years, biology’s inherent complexity and glacial timelines mean that future remains years away.

title: GOP lawmakers push Trump's FDA to make sure clinical trials are diverse
summary: Republican members of Congress have introduced language in a new FDA funding bill requiring that clinical trials enroll participants representative of the patients who will use tested drugs and devices. The effort is designed to protect this principle from the Trump administration's broader crackdown on diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives—HHS began purging all DEI references from agency websites days after Trump took office. The underlying mandate traces to a Biden-era law that has not yet been implemented by the FDA. Supporters distinguish clinical trial enrollment requirements from politically charged DEI programs, arguing they simply ensure trial populations reflect real-world patients. Until Congress acts or HHS implements the rule, sponsors navigating clinical trial diversity face regulatory uncertainty under the new administration.
analysis_notes: Article is a premium/STAT+ teaser behind a paywall; accessible content limited to the lead paragraphs around 200 words. No specific bill name, sponsor names, or concrete trial data were provided. The Biden-era law referenced appears unamed—likely the Clinical Trial Diversity Act or similar HHS mandate. Operators should monitor for formal rulemaking from FDA/HHS to determine actual compliance obligations going forward.

WASHINGTON — Clinical trial policy got caught up in the Trump administration’s attack on policies related to diversity, equity, and inclusion. Republicans in Congress would like to change that.

Days after Trump took office, the Health and Human Services Department began [purging all references to DEI](https://www.statnews.com/2025/01/23/fda-purges-pages-clinical-trial-diversity-after-trump-dei-ban/) from the websites of its agencies. Those efforts were supposed to target DEI initiatives focused on hiring practices, communications, and social issues.

Advertisement

But supporters of clinical trial diversity say the policy has nothing to do with DEI in that sense. Instead, it aims to ensure that clinical trials enroll people who are similar to the patients who would use the drugs and medical devices being tested.

title: Compass says depression drug has long-lasting benefits
summary: The Readout newsletter profiles Compass Psychiatry's psilocybin-based depression treatment, reporting that the therapy produced long-lasting benefits in patients — a notable finding for psychedelic therapeutics given the chronic nature of major depressive disorder. The same edition prominently features Vertex Pharmaceuticals' agreement to acquire Crinetics Pharmaceuticals in a $10 billion deal, described as Vertex's largest-ever acquisition; Crinetics develops drugs for rare endocrine disorders, signaling Vertex's strategic expansion beyond its cystic fibrosis franchise into broader therapeutic areas. This marks one of the most significant M&A transactions in biotech history and suggests continued consolidation pressure on smaller companies with promising pipelines but limited commercial infrastructure.
analysis_notes: Full article text is behind a STAT+ paywall; only headlines, email body preambles, and one snippet were visible. The Compass/psilocybin details (trial phase, patient numbers, effect duration) cannot be confirmed. Vertex-Crinetics $10B deal is corroborated by the newsletter snippet but likely has deeper substance in the full piece.

title: STAT Plus: FDA approves Vera Therapeutics kidney disease treatment
summary: The FDA has approved Trutakna, a new medicine from biotech company Vera Therapeutics, for the treatment of IgA nephropathy (IgAN), a chronic autoimmune condition characterized by the buildup of immune antibodies in the kidneys. IgAN leads to progressive loss of kidney function and can cause organ failure requiring dialysis. The approval represents a significant milestone for Vera Therapeutics as it enters a therapeutic area attracting wide interest from multiple biotech companies. CEO Marshall Fordyce told STAT the company is excited to bring Trutakna to patients, with the interview conducted ahead of the FDA decision.

No clinical data, trial names, regulatory pathway details, market sizing, or deal terms were accessible.

The Food and Drug Administration on Tuesday approved a new medicine from the biotech company Vera Therapeutics for patients with a type of chronic autoimmune kidney disease.

The drug, called Trutakna, was cleared to treat IgA nephropathy, or IgAN, a disease caused by the buildup of immune antibodies in the kidneys. The condition leads to progressive loss of kidney function and potentially organ failure requiring dialysis.

Advertisement

“We’re extremely excited to bring Trutakna to patients,” Vera CEO Marshall Fordyce told STAT, in an interview conducted ahead of the FDA approval.

title: Anatomy of Obesity Drug Tracker: Next-Gen Race to Rival GLP-1
summary: STAT News has updated its comprehensive obesity drug tracker — now tracking more than 170 candidates across the pipeline — as of a June 17, 2025 refresh. The page highlights that Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly currently dominate the market with injectable GLP-1 receptor agonists (Wegovy, Mounjaro), but the competitive landscape is rapidly expanding: dozens of other companies are pursuing oral formulations, longer-lasting dosing intervals, reduced side-effect profiles, and multi-mechanism approaches that go beyond simple weight loss. The update also surfaces key strategic bets shaping the field — including amylin as a potential GLP-1 rival target, RNA interference for gene-silencing-based obesity treatments (e.g., partnerships like Regeneron's approach), and strategies to preserve lean muscle mass during weight reduction. Together, these signals point to an increasingly crowded and mechanistically diverse pipeline competing against theGLP-1 duopoly.
analysis_notes: Article is behind STAT+ paywall; content extracted from pre-paywall text, update notes, linked topic articles, and recommended-story metadata. The tracker's scale (170+ drugs) and the breadth of mechanisms (amylin, siRNA/gene-silencing, muscle-preserving approaches, oral formulations) suggest significant near-term competitive dynamism against the GLP-1 duopoly.

Previous articles describe the bet that_ [_amylin_](https://www.statnews.com/2024/09/05/obesity-drug-tracker-amylin-weight-loss-drugs-novo-nordisk-eli-lilly-zealand-pharma/) _can rival GLP-1 as the next big obesity target; a strategy involving_ [_gene silencing_](https://www.statnews.com/2024/05/23/weight-loss-drugs-rna-interference-gene-silencing-regeneron/)_;__efforts to_ [_preserve muscle_](https://www.statnews.com/2023/11/17/weight-loss-wegovy-zepbound-muscle-loss-stat-obesity-drug-tracker/) _. Also available in a single report: our award-winning_ [_Obesity Revolution_](https://reports.statnews.com/collections/all-reports/products/the-obesity-revolution-how-glp-1-drugs-are-changing-society?variant=40497160257639) _._

The blazing success of weight loss drugs like Wegovy and Mounjaro has electrified the pursuit of new treatments for obesity. For now, Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly are leading the market with injectable drugs that target receptors of the GLP-1 hormone to mimic the hormone’s effects of helping people feel full. But dozens of companies are also jumping into the race, seeing the potential to make millions of dollars if they can bring to market medications that are oral, longer-lasting, avoid side effects, or provide additional benefits besides weight loss.

Advertisement

More than 170 drugs are now in the tracker. Many of these drugs are also targeting receptors of GLP-1, as well as other hormones involved in satiety and metabolism, but some are using entirely novel mechanisms.

title: Vertex acquires Crinetics Pharmaceuticals for $10 billion as biotech M&A booms
summary: Vertex Pharmaceuticals has agreed to acquire Crinetics Pharmaceuticals in a $10 billion all-cash deal, marking the largest acquisition in Vertex's history. The transaction grants Vertex access to Crinetics' blockbuster-commercial drug Palsonify, which treats acromegaly—a rare endocrine disorder—and launched successfully last year. Beyond the already-approved Palsonify, Vertex also acquires a late-stage pipeline candidate targeting congenital adrenal hyperplasia with significant commercial upside if approved. The $10 billion purchase price equates to approximately $85 per share and sent Crinetics shares up 101% in after-hours trading. The deal underscores the current surge in biotech M&A activity as large-cap pharma firms seek to bolster their pipelines through strategic acquisitions rather than organic development.

title: Breakthrough Device Designation FDA Tracker
summary: STAT News has launched an interactive tracker tool mapping the FDA’s Breakthrough Device Program, introduced in 2017 to accelerate regulatory review for promising medical devices. The database covers more than two-thirds of all breakthrough-designated devices to date and allows filtering by condition, clinical area, manufacturer, market pathway, and authorization status. Because the FDA does not publicly disclose breakthrough status except for already-authorized devices, STAT’s database relies on companies that have chosen to share information voluntarily. The tool’s authors note that a breakthrough designation alone does not guarantee eventual FDA authorization or clinical superiority over alternatives. This tracker provides operators and investors with a visible window into a regulatory pathway historically shielded from public scrutiny.
analysis_notes: Article provides no specific biotech drug, trial, or deal content—it is a landing page describing an interactive database tool. No concrete clinical results, approvals, or financial data present. The article’s substantive value lies in explaining FDA regulatory process transparency rather than delivering investable intelligence on a named product or transaction.
raw_markdown:
[Skip to Main Content](https://www.statnews.com/feature/stat-plus/breakthrough-device-designation-fda-tracker/#wrapper)

[![STAT+](https://www.statnews.com/wp-content/themes/stat/images/stat-plus-logo.svg)](https://www.statnews.com/stat-plus/)

The world of medical devices is growing more sprawling every day. Some promising novel ideas can take longer to wind their way through regulatory review and get to patients — so since 2017, the Food and Drug Administration has deployed a program for what it calls “breakthrough devices” to speed the process.

The program has surged significantly in size, but [its implementation remains largely shielded](https://www.statnews.com/2022/04/18/fda-breakthrough-device-designation-investigation/) from the public eye. So STAT identified as many breakthrough-designated devices as possible — more than two-thirds of the total to date — and created a tool to help users understand the FDA’s approach. You can filter by conditions, clinical areas, manufacturer, market pathway, and authorization status.

Advertisement

We’ll be regularly updating the list with new designations as they’re made public. Because the FDA doesn’t disclose breakthrough status except for authorized devices, STAT’s database is sourced directly from companies that have chosen to publicly share it. Keep in mind that breakthroughs aren’t guaranteed to live up to their name — instead, the FDA’s designation indicates that a device, among other criteria, could one day prove to be “more effective” at treating or diagnosing deadly and debilitating conditions. Note that a breakthrough designation has no bearing on whether a device ultimately may be authorized or whether it proves to be more effective than alternatives.
